ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bread food product class; no specific class for white bread with chocolate spread and jam exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and rolls as a category. No specific composite code for white bread with chocolate spread and jam exists; category-level anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for this composite bread-and-spread food item.

serving.description: 2 slices of white bread with chocolate spread and jam
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 80 g (2 slices of white bread, each spread with chocolate spread and jam), commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with chocolate and jam — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'White bread with chocolate and jam' (cr_id F05020123). All per-100g values preserved from the HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for white bread with chocolate and jam spread (closest analogous composite) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: White bread has a well-established GI of approximately 70 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The addition of chocolate spread and jam adds significant sugar but does not substantially lower the GI of the composite; the GI of 70 for white bread is used as the primary anchor. The input record cited GI 40 (low) from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this is not an approved source and the value is inconsistent with the established GI of white bread. GI corrected to 70 (high) per approved source. Glycemic load per 80 g serving: GL = (70 × 41.03 g available carb per 80 g serving) / 100 = approximately 28.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (16.87 g/100 g) from chocolate spread and jam, combined with moderate sodium (319 mg/100 g) and very low potassium (63 mg/100 g), creates an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that elevates blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this food; the high sugar load drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and the low potassium content (63 mg/100 g) fails to counteract sodium's pressor effect — replace with wholegrain bread and an unsweetened nut butter spread.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) white bread base combined with 16.87 g/100 g free sugar from chocolate spread and jam and only 2.49 g/100 g dietary fibre produces a very high glycemic load (GL ~28.7 per serving) causing rapid and sustained postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this food entirely; the combination of refined white bread, chocolate spread, and jam delivers a very high glycemic load per serving and the low fibre content (2.49 g/100 g) provides insufficient buffering of glucose absorption — substitute with wholegrain bread and a low-sugar spread such as natural nut butter.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (16.87 g/100 g) from dual sweet spreads on a refined carbohydrate base promotes dyslipidaemia (elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL), and the moderate sodium (319 mg/100 g) with very low potassium (63 mg/100 g) adds to cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this food; the high sugar and refined carbohydrate load raises triglycerides and suppresses HDL cholesterol, and the low potassium-to-sodium ratio is independently associated with increased cardiovascular event risk — replace with wholegrain bread and a small portion of avocado or unsweetened nut butter.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High free sugar (16.87 g/100 g) from chocolate spread and jam promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; the refined carbohydrate base adds to the glycaemic inflammatory load with no anti-inflammatory nutrients present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food; refined sugar at 16.87 g/100 g is a significant pro-inflammatory trigger and no omega-3, polyphenol, or meaningful fibre content is present to offset the inflammatory burden — replace with wholegrain bread and a small amount of dark chocolate (>70% cocoa) for polyphenol benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with chocolate spread and jam is a common breakfast and snack item widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It consists of sliced white bread — a soft, refined-flour loaf — spread with a chocolate-flavoured spread (such as Nutella or a cocoa-hazelnut spread) and a fruit jam (typically strawberry or mixed fruit). The result is a sweet, soft, and mildly chewy combination with a rich chocolate-fruit flavour profile. Nutritionally, this food is energy-dense at 289 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 51.29 g/100 g and a notably high free sugar content of 16.87 g/100 g contributed by both the chocolate spread and the jam. Dietary fibre is low at 2.49 g/100 g, and the glycemic index is high (GI ~70), resulting in a very high glycemic load per typical serving (~28.7 for an 80 g portion). Sodium is moderate at 319 mg/100 g, while potassium is very low at 63 mg/100 g, creating an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio. Calcium is relatively high at 170 mg/100 g, likely from fortification of the white bread. This food is not suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar, high glycemic load, and pro-inflammatory profile. It is best consumed occasionally and in small portions, ideally replaced with wholegrain bread and lower-sugar spreads for health-conscious individuals.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020123, confidence 0.95). GI corrected from an unapproved embedded source to the established GI of white bread (70, high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous composite and documented. All other HPB-reported values preserved.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for a comparable white bread with chocolate spread and jam composite, as HPB did not report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite bread-and-spread preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026. 3. The input record cited GI 40 (low) from 'Embedded GI Database' — this is not an approved GI source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI has been corrected to 70 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), which is the established GI for white bread. 4. Glycemic load per 80 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (70 × 41.03) / 100 ≈ 28.7. Available carbohydrate per 80 g serving = (51.29 − 2.49) × 0.80 = 38.96 g net carb; total carb basis used: 51.29 × 0.80 = 41.03 g. 5. Halal status assumed based on typical commercial white bread and chocolate spread formulations available in Singapore; kosher status not confirmed. 6. Tree nut allergen flagged as possible due to hazelnut content in common chocolate spreads (e.g., Nutella); actual allergen profile depends on specific brand used.
